speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're doing something a little different. Instead of following one workflow from start to finish, we're building a mental map of GitHub and Git terms you'll hear over and over in open source work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is the episode people can return to when they hear a word like fork, upstream, rebase, or detached HEAD and think, wait, what exactly does that mean again?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. This glossary is meant to be a reference companion for the whole workshop, and really for all chapters. If a term feels fuzzy, this is where you come back to ground yourself.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And because our audience includes blind and low-vision learners, let's start with the navigation tips too, not just the definitions.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If you're using a screen reader, the glossary is designed so you can move by headings. Jumping by heading level is a fast way to move term by term, especially where each term is a level-three heading. You can also open your screen reader's elements list, like NVDA plus F7 or Insert plus F6 in some screen readers, to browse all headings at once and filter by typing a few letters.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if you know the term but not the category, the alphabetical quick reference at the bottom is often the fastest path.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. For low-vision users, increasing zoom and scanning the bold term headings can make the page much easier to browse. The alphabetical table is also useful at high zoom because each row stands on its own. And for sighted users, the categories can help you narrow the search, while Control-F is still a great shortcut for everyone.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's start with the building blocks. First up: repository, often shortened to repo.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is one of those words people hear immediately, but it helps to define it carefully.

speaker: ALEX
text: A repository is the container for an entire project. It holds the files, folders, documentation, and the full history of changes. A good analogy is a project folder with a time machine built in. You don't just have the current version of the files. You have a recorded history of how they changed over time.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And on GitHub, a repository lives at a web address like github dot com slash owner slash repo name.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And a few related terms matter here. A remote repository usually means the copy hosted on GitHub. A local repository is the copy on your computer. Upstream is the original project you forked from. And a fork is your own copy of someone else's repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That naturally leads to organization, or org.

speaker: ALEX
text: An organization is a shared GitHub account for groups of people. Many open source projects live under organizations rather than personal accounts. In a URL like github dot com slash microsoft slash vscode, microsoft is the organization and vscode is the repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if a learner sees a project under an org, that usually means multiple maintainers or teams may be involved.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's often true.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's define fork, because that one can feel abstract at first.

speaker: ALEX
text: A fork is a personal copy of someone else's repository that lives in your own GitHub account. It includes the files and the history. Changes you make in your fork do not automatically affect the original repository. If you want your changes considered by the original project, you open a pull request.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the reason people fork is simple: most open source repositories do not give random contributors direct write access.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Forking gives you a safe place to work freely.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, then what is a remote?

speaker: ALEX
text: A remote is a named connection from your local Git repository to a repository hosted somewhere else, usually on GitHub. Your local repository can have more than one remote. The most common two are origin and upstream.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Origin usually points to your fork, and upstream usually points to the original repository you forked from.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. If you run a command like git push origin main, you're pushing your local main branch to the remote named origin.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if someone wants to inspect their remotes, the command is git remote space dash v.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. That shows the remote names and URLs. And to add a remote, you might use something like git remote add upstream followed by the URL of the original repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So origin is really a special case of remote.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's a great way to say it. Origin is the default name Git gives the repository you cloned from. If you cloned your fork, then origin points to your fork on GitHub. Commands like git push and git pull often default to origin unless you specify a different remote.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's talk about dot gitignore, because this one protects people from a lot of headaches.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. A.gitignore file lives at the root of your repository and tells Git which files or folders to ignore. In other words, Git will not track or commit those files if they are untracked.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there are a few common categories of things people usually want to ignore.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Dependencies, like node underscore modules or vendor folders. Environment variables, like.env files that may contain secrets. Build outputs, like dist or build folders and sometimes log files. Operating system files, such as.DS underscore Store on macOS or Thumbs dot db on Windows. And IDE files, such as.vscode or.idea.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Important accessibility note for understanding this concept:.gitignore is not just housekeeping. It's often a security and collaboration tool.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. It helps keep secrets out of Git, keeps generated files from cluttering history, and avoids editor-specific noise that doesn't belong in the project.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's one important gotcha.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes..gitignore only applies to untracked files. If a file was already committed, adding it to.gitignore does not magically remove it from version control. You first have to stop tracking it, often with git rm dash dash cached filename, then add it to.gitignore and commit that change.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's such a common beginner surprise.

speaker: ALEX
text: It really is.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Next term: clone.

speaker: ALEX
text: Cloning means copying a repository from GitHub to your local computer so you can work on it in VS Code or another editor. When you clone, you get both the files and the full history.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And this is where people mix up fork and clone.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Fork is your copy on GitHub. Clone is your copy on your computer. In a typical open source workflow, you fork first on GitHub, then clone your fork locally.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now branch.

speaker: ALEX
text: A branch is a separate line of development inside a repository. The main branch, often called main and in some older projects master, is usually the stable primary branch. When you want to add a feature or fix a bug, you create another branch so your work stays isolated until it's ready.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like the idea of branches as parallel timelines.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's the classic mental model. They can evolve independently, then be merged later. Common names include main, develop, feature slash something, fix slash something, or docs slash something.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So branch names often communicate purpose.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then we have commit.

speaker: ALEX
text: A commit is a saved snapshot of your changes at a moment in time. Each commit has a message describing what changed and why, a unique SHA hash, the author and timestamp, and the actual additions and deletions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And good commit messages are usually written in the imperative mood. So, Fix typo in README, not Fixed typo.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. A strong commit message helps future readers understand intent. Sometimes the message also includes a longer body explaining context, and it may reference an issue number, like Fixes number 42.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Last building-block term in this group: diff.

speaker: ALEX
text: A diff shows the difference between two versions of a file. Added lines are typically shown in green with a plus sign. Removed lines are shown in red with a minus sign. Unchanged lines appear around them for context.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And even if a learner can't see those colors, the conceptual point still matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Whether you're using visual diff views or screen reader-friendly text output, you're still interpreting additions, deletions, and context. Reading diffs is a core skill for code review. When you open the Files Changed tab in a pull request, you're reading a diff.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's shift to collaboration workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Starting with issues.

speaker: ALEX
text: An issue is a discussion item in a GitHub repository. Issues can report bugs, request features, ask questions, discuss ideas, or track work. Each issue gets a number, like number 42, and can include labels, assignees, milestones, and comments.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And in many projects, issues are the starting point for contribution.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. It's often best practice to find or open an issue before changing code. That helps you avoid duplicated work and align with maintainers.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then comes the pull request, or PR.

speaker: ALEX
text: A pull request is a proposal to merge changes from one branch into another. You're essentially saying, here is my work, please review it and consider bringing it into the target branch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: A PR shows the source branch, the target branch, the diff, a description, and the discussion around the change.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. And the phrase pull request comes from the idea that you're requesting maintainers to pull your changes into their project.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then reviewers step in.

speaker: ALEX
text: That process is called code review. One or more collaborators read the PR and provide feedback. They may leave comments, approve the change, or request changes before merge.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And good reviews are not just technically correct. They're kind, specific, and constructive.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Code review is communication as much as evaluation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's define merge.

speaker: ALEX
text: Merging means combining changes from one branch into another. Once a PR is approved, a maintainer may merge it. Common strategies include a merge commit, which creates a new commit recording the merge; squash and merge, which combines all PR commits into one; and rebase and merge, which replays the commits on top of the target branch for a linear history.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then the scary phrase: merge conflict.

speaker: ALEX
text: A merge conflict happens when two branches changed the same part of the same file in different ways, and Git can't decide which version to keep. It stops and asks a human to resolve the conflict.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's when you may see conflict markers in the file.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. You'll see markers that separate your current branch content from the incoming branch content. You resolve the conflict by editing the file so it contains the correct final version, removing the markers, and then committing the resolution.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So a conflict is not Git failing. It's Git asking for help where ambiguity exists.

speaker: ALEX
text: Beautifully put.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now upstream.

speaker: ALEX
text: Upstream refers to the original repository you forked from. If you have a fork, your changes often go from your local clone to your fork, and then through a PR toward the original project. But updates from the original project can also move the other direction into your fork when you sync with upstream.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So upstream is about the source project, while origin is usually your fork.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then some organizing terms: label, milestone, and project.

speaker: ALEX
text: A label is a colored tag used to categorize issues and pull requests. Labels might indicate bug, documentation, accessibility, good first issue, help wanted, or priority.

speaker: JAMIE
text: A milestone groups related issues or PRs around a goal or release.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. And GitHub Projects is a planning tool, often board-like, used to track work across issues and PRs. Think status columns, planning views, and a shared work dashboard.

speaker: ALEX
text: Next are people and roles.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Maintainer is probably the most important one to define.

speaker: ALEX
text: A maintainer is a person with responsibility for the repository. They often have write or admin access and make decisions about issues, pull requests, releases, and direction.

speaker: JAMIE
text: A contributor is broader.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. A contributor is anyone who contributes to the project. That could mean code, documentation, design, bug reports, testing, accessibility feedback, translations, discussions, and more.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then triage.

speaker: ALEX
text: Triage is the work of reviewing and categorizing incoming issues or pull requests. That may include adding labels, asking clarifying questions, closing duplicates, or helping route work to the right people.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And collaborator?

speaker: ALEX
text: A collaborator is someone who has been granted direct access to work in a repository, usually with some level of write permission, even if they are not a full maintainer.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The glossary also includes common abbreviations and slang. We may not have every single informal phrase spelled out in the source excerpt we saw, but let's cover the style of language learners will hear.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good idea. You'll regularly hear repo for repository, org for organization, PR for pull request, and SHA pronounced either as individual letters or sometimes just called a hash. You'll also hear people say push, pull, fetch, merge, squash, rebase, and sync as shorthand for common Git actions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And it's okay if this language feels dense at first. Fluency comes from repetition.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now we move into more technical Git and GitHub concepts.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's start with HEAD, because that name sounds strange.

speaker: ALEX
text: HEAD is a pointer to your current location in Git, usually the latest commit on the branch you're currently on. If you're on a branch, HEAD generally points to that branch tip.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So HEAD answers the question, where am I right now in the commit history?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then what is detached HEAD?

speaker: ALEX
text: Detached HEAD means HEAD is pointing directly to a commit instead of to a branch name. This often happens if you check out a specific commit, a tag, or something other than a branch tip.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Why does that matter?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because if you make commits while detached, those commits are not attached to a named branch. They can become hard to find later if you move away. That's the big practical meaning.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So why it happens is usually that a person checked out a commit or tag directly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. What it means is: you're looking at a snapshot, not working on a normal branch. And how to fix it is usually to create or switch to a branch. If you want to keep work you made there, create a new branch from that point so the commits have a name and a stable reference.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's much less scary when explained plainly.

speaker: ALEX
text: It really is.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Next: stash.

speaker: ALEX
text: A stash is a temporary shelf for uncommitted changes. If you're in the middle of work and need to switch branches or handle something urgent, stash lets you set aside your changes without making a commit yet.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What's a common workflow?

speaker: ALEX
text: Imagine you're working on branch A and you suddenly need to do something on main, or on another branch. You can stash your uncommitted changes, switch branches, do the urgent task, then come back and reapply the stash.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there are specific stash commands people should recognize.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Common ones include git stash to save changes, git stash list to see saved stashes, git stash apply to reapply one while keeping it in the stash list, and git stash pop to reapply it and remove it from the list.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Rebase is one of the most confusing terms for beginners.

speaker: ALEX
text: So let's frame it carefully. Merge and rebase can both integrate changes from one line of development into another, but they do it differently.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Merge preserves the branching history and creates a merge commit.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Rebase, by contrast, replays your commits on top of another base commit. The result is a cleaner, more linear history, but it rewrites commit history.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's the key tradeoff.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. When to use rebase? Often when you're updating your local feature branch to sit on top of the latest main before opening or updating a PR, especially in projects that prefer linear history.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And when should you not rebase?

speaker: ALEX
text: Do not casually rebase shared public history that other people are already building on. Because rebase changes commit identities, it can disrupt collaborators.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What's a basic rebase workflow?

speaker: ALEX
text: You update your knowledge of the remote branch, then rebase your current branch onto the newer target branch. If conflicts appear, you fix the conflicts, continue the rebase, and test your result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So that omitted heading about fixing conflicts still matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Definitely. Conflicts during rebase are resolved much like merge conflicts: edit the files, remove conflict markers, then continue the process.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then there's cherry-pick.

speaker: ALEX
text: Cherry-pick means taking one specific commit and applying it onto your current branch. Instead of merging a whole branch, you choose a single commit by its SHA.

speaker: JAMIE
text: When would you use it?

speaker: ALEX
text: When one isolated fix belongs on another branch too. For example, maybe a bug fix was made on a feature branch and you want that exact fix on a release branch without bringing over all the other work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Fetch versus pull is another pair that gets blurred together.

speaker: ALEX
text: Fetch downloads new information from a remote without merging it into your current branch. Pull usually means fetch plus merge, or fetch plus rebase, depending on configuration.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So when should learners use fetch?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use fetch when you want to see what's changed remotely without changing your working branch yet. It's a cautious, inspect-first move.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And pull?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use pull when you're ready to bring remote changes directly into your local branch in one step.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now the term everyone approaches with caution: force push.

speaker: ALEX
text: Force push overwrites the remote branch reference with your local branch history, even if that means discarding the remote's previous history for that branch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Why would anyone need that?

speaker: ALEX
text: Most commonly after rewriting history locally, such as after a rebase or an interactive cleanup of commits. Since the commit identities changed, a normal push may be rejected.

speaker: JAMIE
text: When is force push okay?

speaker: ALEX
text: Usually only on your own branch, especially a personal feature branch where you understand the consequences and no one else is relying on that exact remote history.

speaker: JAMIE
text: When is it never okay?

speaker: ALEX
text: Never force push shared protected branches like main in a collaborative project unless there is an extraordinary, explicitly coordinated administrative reason. For ordinary work, that's off limits.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the safer alternative is force with lease.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Dash dash force with lease checks that the remote branch is still in the state you expect before overwriting it. It's safer because it helps prevent accidentally deleting someone else's newer work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's define SHA or hash more directly.

speaker: ALEX
text: A SHA, often just called a hash, is the unique identifier for a commit or other Git object. Think of it like a fingerprint. Even a short abbreviated SHA is often enough to identify a commit in conversation or commands.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Tag and release are related but not identical.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. A tag is a permanent named reference to a specific commit, often used for version markers like v1 point 0 point 0. A release is a GitHub concept built around a versioned snapshot, often with release notes and downloadable assets. Releases commonly point to tags.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now actions, workflows, and CI slash CD.

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub Actions is GitHub's automation system. A workflow is an automated process defined in files in the repository, often triggered by events like pushes or pull requests. CI means continuous integration, usually automated testing and checks on new changes. CD can mean continuous delivery or deployment, where software is packaged or released automatically.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if a PR runs tests automatically, that's usually a workflow in Actions acting as CI.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And those results often appear as status checks.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. A status check is an automated pass or fail result shown on a pull request or commit. It might report that tests passed, linting failed, builds succeeded, or accessibility checks found a problem.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Webhook sounds more infrastructure-heavy.

speaker: ALEX
text: A webhook is a way for GitHub to send event data to another service when something happens, such as a push, issue comment, or PR update. It's part of how GitHub can integrate with outside tools.

speaker: JAMIE
text: GitHub Discussions?

speaker: ALEX
text: That's a dedicated discussion space for conversation that isn't necessarily an issue or a pull request. It's useful for questions, ideas, community support, and longer-form project discussion.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Profile is simpler, but still worth naming.

speaker: ALEX
text: Your GitHub profile is your public account presence: your repositories, contribution activity, profile README if you have one, and other information you choose to share.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And GitHub Copilot?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub Copilot is an AI coding assistant integrated into tools like editors and GitHub features. It can suggest code and text, but it's important to treat those suggestions as drafts to review carefully, not as unquestioned truth.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The source also points to community files and quick reference material.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. One purpose of the glossary is not just long-form explanation but also quick lookup. The alphabetical quick reference is there for flat A to Z scanning when you already know the term and just need the meaning fast.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that can be especially useful for screen reader users who want a simpler lookup structure, and for low-vision users at high zoom where compact self-contained rows can be easier to navigate.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The glossary serves both learning and reference.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There were also a few extra terms visible in the quick reference list that are useful to name briefly, even if they weren't expanded earlier in the body.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good catch. README is usually the main documentation file introducing a repository. Review is the formal feedback process on a pull request, which we discussed under code review. Squash merge is the strategy that combines all commits in a PR into one when merging. Pull is the command that fetches remote changes and integrates them. Wiki is a multi-page documentation area some repositories use.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the repeated items in the table, like tag, triage, and upstream, reinforce that these terms come up a lot.

speaker: ALEX
text: They do.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we wrap up, let's include the administrative notes too.

speaker: ALEX
text: This glossary points learners to an audio companion, which is exactly what we're creating here, and it also names an authoritative source for facts: GitHub Docs, especially the glossary and related documentation. The GitHub Changelog is also useful when details evolve over time.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if a learner ever finds that a feature has changed since the workshop material was written, the official docs and changelog are the place to verify current behavior.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The section-level source map in the written material is there for that purpose: to connect major sections of the glossary with authoritative references.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the written appendix also points forward to the next appendix, the screen reader cheat sheet, while reminding learners that this glossary supports the GitHub fundamentals chapter and really all chapters.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's right. If you remember nothing else, remember the structure: a repository contains the project and history, a fork is your GitHub copy, a clone is your local copy, branches isolate work, commits save snapshots, diffs show changes, issues track discussion, pull requests propose changes, reviews refine them, and merges combine them.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then as your confidence grows, the more advanced terms like HEAD, stash, rebase, cherry-pick, fetch, pull, and force push start fitting into that bigger map.

speaker: ALEX
text: Well said. Keep this glossary close. Revisit it often. Fluency in Git and GitHub is not about memorizing everything at once. It's about hearing the terms repeatedly in meaningful workflows until they become familiar.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if you're learning with a screen reader or at high zoom, remember: good navigation strategies are part of technical fluency too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Thanks for learning with us.
